title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
oneLiner: Acute immune-mediated polyneuropathy after infection; ascending paralysis ± respiratory failure – needs early recognition for monitoring.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Respiratory failure** – single bedside finding: forced vital capacity (FVC) < 20 mL/kg or single breath count < 15.
- **Dysautonomia** – single finding: wide pulse pressure + episodic bradycardia/hypertension (vagal/ sympathetic storm). Precedes arrest.
- **Airway obstruction from bulbar palsy** – ask to swallow water; if choking or weak cough → risk of aspiration.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Ascending symmetrical flaccid paralysis (typically days to weeks after GI/respiratory infection)
  - Paraesthesiae in hands/feet (stocking-glove)
  - Back pain, myalgia
  - Cranial nerve involvement: facial diplegia, ptosis, bulbar weakness (dysphagia, dysarthria)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Areflexia/hyporeflexia (early)
  - Symmetrical weakness (proximal and distal)
  - Sensory loss: light touch/vibration more than proprioception
  - ± autonomic: postural hypotension, ileus, urinary retention, arrhythmias
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Spinal cord compression (sensory level, sphincter involvement, brisk reflexes → need MRI urgently)
  - Myasthenia gravis (fatiguability, no sensory loss, preserved reflexes, positive anti-AChR)
  - Brainstem stroke (sudden onset, crossed signs, vertigo, dysarthria, imaging acute)
  - Transverse myelitis (sensory level, sphincters, CSF pleocytosis)
  - Botulism (descending paralysis, dilated pupils, impaired accommodation, no sensory loss)
  - Tick paralysis (tick found, improves within 24 h of removal)
  - Acute intermittent porphyria (abdominal pain, psychosis, seizures)
- **Splitter: sensory loss + areflexia → GBS; pure motor + fatiguability → MG**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - FVC or single breath count (normal > 30) – do q4-6h
  - Swallow test (water or saliva)
  - Lying/standing BP (autonomic instability)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - Anti-GQ1b (if Miller Fisher variant: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia)
  - Serology: Campylobacter, CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, HIV, Zika/Dengue (travel)
  - Consider ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-AChR if atypical
- **Imaging:**
  - MRI whole spine (with gadolinium) to exclude cord compression or cauda equina – do **before LP** if focal signs/sensory level
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture: CSF protein elevated (albuminocytologic dissociation) – typically > 1 g/L, with normal cell count
  - Nerve conduction studies/EMG: confirm demyelinating pattern (may take 1-2 weeks to show)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Brighton criteria (for research/audit). Bedside: ascending weakness + areflexia + supportive CSF → start treatment.
- **Never-miss test:** MRI spine to rule out compressive myelopathy – missing it risks irreversible paralysis if delay.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - Airway assessment: FVC < 20 mL/kg → call anaesthetics/ICU for elective intubation
  - If bulbar: nil oral, nurse head-up, suction available
  - Monitor BP/HR continuously
  - If stable: arrange transfer to HDU/ICU (even mild cases can deteriorate rapidly)
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Start IVIG (0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days) – best started within 2 weeks of onset
  - Plasma exchange (alternative, or if IVIG fails) – requires central line, usually 5 sessions
  - Do **not** give steroids (no benefit, may worsen outcome)
  - Supportive: physiotherapy, speech therapy, pressure area care, bowel/bladder management
  - Pain: neuropathic agents (gabapentin, amitriptyline) – start low, titrate
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - Neurologist follow-up at 4-6 weeks for NCS
  - Rehabilitation: physio, OT, psychology
  - Vaccination: check if appropriate (avoid live vaccines for 6-12 months after IVIG)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1-2 L/day (caution if autonomic instability)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (once clotting normal, no active bleeding)
  - Diet: NBM if bulbar; otherwise soft diet (risk of aspiration)
  - Observations: hourly for first 12 h, then q4h: NEWS2, FVC, BP (lying/standing)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - IVIG (human normal immunoglobulin) 0.4 g/kg/day IV over 4-8 h for 5 days (total 2 g/kg)
  - Pre-hydrate with 0.9% saline, give slowly (0.5-1 mL/kg/h for first 30 min, then increase)
- **Alternative:**
  - Plasma exchange – 5-7 sessions over 10-14 days (check local protocol)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - IVIG contraindicated in IgA deficiency (check IgA before infusion – life-threatening anaphylaxis)
  - Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) contraindicated for 12 months after IVIG
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - IVIG: caution in renal impairment → reduce infusion rate; consider pre-hydration
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - IVIG too rapid → acute renal failure, thrombosis, or anaphylaxis. **Always start infusion slowly and check IgA before first dose.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure (most common cause death)
  - Dysautonomia: severe bradycardia, asystole, hypertension, ileus, urinary retention
  - Pain: neuropathic + musculoskeletal (common)
  - DVT/PE
  - Infection (ventilator-associated, aspiration)
- **Chronic:**
  - Fatigue (very common)
  - Residual weakness/wasting (20% persistent disability)
  - Chronic pain
  - Guillain-Barré recurrence (<5%)
sections.Prognosis: - 5-10% mortality (respiratory/autonomic). ~80% recover fully or with mild residual. Poor prognostic factors: age > 40, rapid progression (< 7 days), need for ventilation, preceding Campylobacter diarrhoea, axonal variant.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do** bedside FVC and single breath count q4-6h – if dropping > 30% from baseline, escalate.
- **Do** check for autonomic instability: measure lying & standing BP, watch for bradycardia during suction.
- **Do** start IVIG as soon as diagnosis is likely – don't wait for NCS confirmation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not** give steroids – they delay recovery and increase relapse risk.
- **Do not** assume stable because can walk – weakness can worsen rapidly over hours.
- **Do not** perform LP **after** starting IVIG (it's okay but may miss other causes). Always do LP first if possible.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Any difficulty breathing or swallowing
  - New weakness or falls
  - Chest pain, palpitations, severe headache (IVIG side effect)
  - inability to care for self (urinary retention, falls)
- **Follow-up:** Neurology clinic in 4-6 weeks; GP for VTE review; physiotherapy referral.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ICU/senior if:**
  - FVC < 20 mL/kg or drop > 30% from previous
  - Swallow unsafe and unable to protect airway
  - NEWS2 > 5 or increasing
  - Any decline in consciousness
  - Acute arrhythmia or severe BP instability
- **Crash call** for respiratory arrest, asystole, or ventricular arrhythmia.

rawMarkdown:
## Guillain-Barré Syndrome

**One-liner:** Acute immune-mediated polyneuropathy after infection; ascending paralysis ± respiratory failure – needs early recognition for monitoring.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Respiratory failure** – single bedside finding: forced vital capacity (FVC) < 20 mL/kg or single breath count < 15.
- **Dysautonomia** – single finding: wide pulse pressure + episodic bradycardia/hypertension (vagal/ sympathetic storm). Precedes arrest.
- **Airway obstruction from bulbar palsy** – ask to swallow water; if choking or weak cough → risk of aspiration.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Ascending symmetrical flaccid paralysis (typically days to weeks after GI/respiratory infection)
  - Paraesthesiae in hands/feet (stocking-glove)
  - Back pain, myalgia
  - Cranial nerve involvement: facial diplegia, ptosis, bulbar weakness (dysphagia, dysarthria)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Areflexia/hyporeflexia (early)
  - Symmetrical weakness (proximal and distal)
  - Sensory loss: light touch/vibration more than proprioception
  - ± autonomic: postural hypotension, ileus, urinary retention, arrhythmias

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Spinal cord compression (sensory level, sphincter involvement, brisk reflexes → need MRI urgently)
  - Myasthenia gravis (fatiguability, no sensory loss, preserved reflexes, positive anti-AChR)
  - Brainstem stroke (sudden onset, crossed signs, vertigo, dysarthria, imaging acute)
  - Transverse myelitis (sensory level, sphincters, CSF pleocytosis)
  - Botulism (descending paralysis, dilated pupils, impaired accommodation, no sensory loss)
  - Tick paralysis (tick found, improves within 24 h of removal)
  - Acute intermittent porphyria (abdominal pain, psychosis, seizures)
- **Splitter: sensory loss + areflexia → GBS; pure motor + fatiguability → MG**

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - FVC or single breath count (normal > 30) – do q4-6h
  - Swallow test (water or saliva)
  - Lying/standing BP (autonomic instability)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR
  - Anti-GQ1b (if Miller Fisher variant: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia)
  - Serology: Campylobacter, CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, HIV, Zika/Dengue (travel)
  - Consider ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-AChR if atypical
- **Imaging:**
  - MRI whole spine (with gadolinium) to exclude cord compression or cauda equina – do **before LP** if focal signs/sensory level
- **Special tests:**
  - Lumbar puncture: CSF protein elevated (albuminocytologic dissociation) – typically > 1 g/L, with normal cell count
  - Nerve conduction studies/EMG: confirm demyelinating pattern (may take 1-2 weeks to show)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Brighton criteria (for research/audit). Bedside: ascending weakness + areflexia + supportive CSF → start treatment.
- **Never-miss test:** MRI spine to rule out compressive myelopathy – missing it risks irreversible paralysis if delay.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - Airway assessment: FVC < 20 mL/kg → call anaesthetics/ICU for elective intubation
  - If bulbar: nil oral, nurse head-up, suction available
  - Monitor BP/HR continuously
  - If stable: arrange transfer to HDU/ICU (even mild cases can deteriorate rapidly)
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Start IVIG (0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days) – best started within 2 weeks of onset
  - Plasma exchange (alternative, or if IVIG fails) – requires central line, usually 5 sessions
  - Do **not** give steroids (no benefit, may worsen outcome)
  - Supportive: physiotherapy, speech therapy, pressure area care, bowel/bladder management
  - Pain: neuropathic agents (gabapentin, amitriptyline) – start low, titrate
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - Neurologist follow-up at 4-6 weeks for NCS
  - Rehabilitation: physio, OT, psychology
  - Vaccination: check if appropriate (avoid live vaccines for 6-12 months after IVIG)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1-2 L/day (caution if autonomic instability)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (once clotting normal, no active bleeding)
  - Diet: NBM if bulbar; otherwise soft diet (risk of aspiration)
  - Observations: hourly for first 12 h, then q4h: NEWS2, FVC, BP (lying/standing)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - IVIG (human normal immunoglobulin) 0.4 g/kg/day IV over 4-8 h for 5 days (total 2 g/kg)
  - Pre-hydrate with 0.9% saline, give slowly (0.5-1 mL/kg/h for first 30 min, then increase)
- **Alternative:**
  - Plasma exchange – 5-7 sessions over 10-14 days (check local protocol)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - IVIG contraindicated in IgA deficiency (check IgA before infusion – life-threatening anaphylaxis)
  - Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) contraindicated for 12 months after IVIG
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - IVIG: caution in renal impairment → reduce infusion rate; consider pre-hydration
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - IVIG too rapid → acute renal failure, thrombosis, or anaphylaxis. **Always start infusion slowly and check IgA before first dose.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure (most common cause death)
  - Dysautonomia: severe bradycardia, asystole, hypertension, ileus, urinary retention
  - Pain: neuropathic + musculoskeletal (common)
  - DVT/PE
  - Infection (ventilator-associated, aspiration)
- **Chronic:**
  - Fatigue (very common)
  - Residual weakness/wasting (20% persistent disability)
  - Chronic pain
  - Guillain-Barré recurrence (<5%)

### Prognosis
- 5-10% mortality (respiratory/autonomic). ~80% recover fully or with mild residual. Poor prognostic factors: age > 40, rapid progression (< 7 days), need for ventilation, preceding Campylobacter diarrhoea, axonal variant.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do** bedside FVC and single breath count q4-6h – if dropping > 30% from baseline, escalate.
- **Do** check for autonomic instability: measure lying & standing BP, watch for bradycardia during suction.
- **Do** start IVIG as soon as diagnosis is likely – don't wait for NCS confirmation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not** give steroids – they delay recovery and increase relapse risk.
- **Do not** assume stable because can walk – weakness can worsen rapidly over hours.
- **Do not** perform LP **after** starting IVIG (it's okay but may miss other causes). Always do LP first if possible.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Any difficulty breathing or swallowing
  - New weakness or falls
  - Chest pain, palpitations, severe headache (IVIG side effect)
  - inability to care for self (urinary retention, falls)
- **Follow-up:** Neurology clinic in 4-6 weeks; GP for VTE review; physiotherapy referral.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ICU/senior if:**
  - FVC < 20 mL/kg or drop > 30% from previous
  - Swallow unsafe and unable to protect airway
  - NEWS2 > 5 or increasing
  - Any decline in consciousness
  - Acute arrhythmia or severe BP instability
- **Crash call** for respiratory arrest, asystole, or ventricular arrhythmia.
